Singapore March Manufacturing PMI Indicates Expansion But at Slower Pace
data.description:
By Amanda Lee


SINGAPORE--A key gauge of Singapore's manufacturing activity expanded for the eighth consecutive month in March, albeit at a slower pace amid the Middle East conflict.

The purchasing managers index compiled by the Singapore Institute of Purchasing and Materials Management edged down to 50.5 last month from 50.6 in February. A reading above 50 indicates expansion, while one below signals contraction.

The reading showed a slower expansion in new orders, new exports, factory output and employment, SIPMM said Thursday.

"It is encouraging to note that the latest PMI readings continue to reflect a positive outlook for the overall manufacturing sector, despite heightened geopolitical uncertainties arising from the U.S.-Iran conflict," said Stephen Poh, executive director at SIPMM.

The PMI for electronics, which accounts for about one-third of Singapore's manufacturing output, edged up to 51.4 in March from 51.3 in February, thanks to a faster expansion in new orders, new exports, factory output, input purchases and employment.

The electronics sector remains a key driver of the latest PMI's expansion, driven by strong global demand for semiconductors, artificial-intelligence hardware, and data center components, Poh said.

Singapore's small, open economy makes it vulnerable to external shocks, such as the conflict, which has threatened supply chain disruptions.

The city-state's growth and trade are expected to weaken if the Middle East tensions continue throughout the first half of this year, RHB Bank's Barnabas Gan said in a recent report.

Higher global oil prices are likely to weigh on Singapore's trade balance, as it would raise input and energy costs for firms, especially in manufacturing, transport and petrochemical, Gan added.


Write to Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-02-26 0914ET

data.description: The Board of Directors of The Bank of Nova Scotia has authorized a buyback plan on April 2, 2026.

data.title: The Bank of Nova Scotia announces an Equity Buyback for 15,000,000 shares, representing 1.22% of its issued share capital.
data.description:
The Bank of Nova Scotia (TSX:BNS) announces a share repurchase program. Under the normal course issuer bid, the company will repurchase up to 15,000,000 shares, representing 1.21% of its issued share capital. All the shares purchased under the bid will be cancelled.

The bid will expire on April 6, 2027. As of March 24, 2026, the company has 1,231,433,660 common shares outstanding.

Tesla's first-quarter deliveries miss estimates as tax credit expiry weighs
data.description:
April 2 (Reuters) - Tesla missed Wall Street expectations for first-quarter deliveries on Thursday, as the expiry of U.S. tax credits on the purchase of electric vehicles weighed on demand.

The company delivered 358,023 vehicles in the January-March period, down 14.4% from the fourth quarter, up 6.3% from a year earlier.

Analysts on average had expected deliveries of 368,903 vehicles, according to Visible Alpha data.

(Reporting by Akash Sriram in Bengaluru; Editing by Devika Syamnath)

Trade Deficit Grew in February as Policy Continued to Swing
data.description:
By Matt Grossman and Anthony DeBarros


The U.S. trade deficit increased in February, continuing a bumpy stretch for international flows of goods amid fast-changing U.S. policy.

February imports were $372.1 billion, up 4.3% from January. U.S. exports in February increased slightly less, by 4.2%, to $314.8 billion.

That yielded a February trade deficit of $57.4 billion, up 4.9% from the January deficit. Analysts polled by The Wall Street Journal were expecting a bigger deficit of $62 billion.

The deficit for the first two months of the year is 55% smaller than the same period in 2025, when firms were rushing to import goods ahead of tariffs.

Steep new tariffs rocked American supply chains last year, and U.S. businesses are still facing a fast-changing policy landscape. On Feb. 20, the Supreme Court overturned what had been a primary leg of President Trump's tariff agenda, broad global duties imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

The court found that Trump's tariffs went too far without clear authority from Congress.

Trump responded by quickly enacting new tariffs under a different statute--but one whose authority will expire in July. Meanwhile, the Trump administration is seeking other ways to add additional tariffs. Meanwhile, previous duties that weren't overturned by the Supreme Court remain in place.

Though trade patterns have shifted more abruptly from month to month amid these rapid rulebook changes, recent monthly trade-deficit levels have been roughly consistent with those recorded during much of the Biden administration.


Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com and Anthony DeBarros at anthony.debarros@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-02-26 0900ET

data.title: U.S. Jobless Claims Fell Unexpectedly Last Week
data.description:
By Ed Frankl


U.S. jobless claims declined last week, with little sign so far that the rise in global energy prices prompted by the conflict in the Middle East is scarring employment.

The number of people who filed for unemployment benefits was 202,000 in the week through March 28, down 9,000 from the previous week's upwardly revised level of 211,000, the Labor Department said Thursday. Economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal were expecting to see a higher 212,000 new claims.

Continuing claims, which scale with the total size of the unemployed population, rose to 1.841 million in the week through March 21, versus 1.816 million a week earlier. The four-week moving average, at 1.839 million was the lowest level since September 2024, the Labor Department said. The data on continuing claims lag the initial-claims data by a week.

The figures come a day ahead of the government's official March jobs report. Economists polled by the WSJ estimate 59,000 new jobs, recovering from the 92,000 job losses reported for February, alongside an unchanged unemployment rate at 4.4%.


Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

KKR raises $23 billion for North America private equity fund
data.description:
April 2 (Reuters) - Investment firm KKR said on Thursday it had raised $23 billion for its North America-focused private equity fund, the largest such fund the firm has devoted to the region.

The raise highlights continued investor demand for private market bets, as companies remain private longer and seek to avoid the volatility of public markets.

Some of the world's most valuable companies, including OpenAI and prediction market platform Kalshi, have opted to stay private while still pulling in billions.

The KKR North America Fund XIV, or NAX4, will target opportunistic private equity investments across North America.

Over the past decade, NAX4's three predecessor funds delivered a gross return of 23%. KKR's private equity assets under management have since risen to about $229 billion, roughly doubling since 2020.

The fund attracted both new and existing investors, including public and private pension plans, sovereign wealth funds, insurers, endowments and foundations, and private wealth platforms, KKR said in a statement.

(Reporting by Pragyan Kalita in Bengaluru; Editing by Tasim Zahid)

Chicago Fed Model Projects Uptick in March Unemployment
data.description:
By Matt Grossman


Unemployment may have ticked up to 4.5% last month, according to an economic model published by the Chicago Fed.

The reserve bank's job-market model projects that the unemployment rate inched higher to 4.49% in March, versus the 4.44% recorded in February. That slight increase would be enough to lift the rounded figure by a tenth of a percentage point.

The Chicago Fed's model is based on a blend of public- and private-sector data. The official figure from the Labor Department, which is based on a large survey of households, arrives on Friday.

Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal have projected unemployment will hold steady at 4.4%.


Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires
